Marilyn McCord Adams and Stewart
I--Marilyn McCord Adams
I
Introduction. Over the past thirty years, analytic philosophers of religion have defined 'the problem of evil' in terms of the prima facie difficulty in consistently maintaining
God exists, and is omnipotent, omniscient, and
perfectly good
and
Evil exists.
In a crisp and classic article 'Evil and Omnipotence',' J. L. Mackie emphasized that the problem is not that (1) and (2) are logically inconsistent by themselves, but that they together with quasi-logical rules formulating attribute-analyses--such as
) A perfectly good being would always eliminate evil so far as it could,
and
) There are no limits to what an omnipotent being can do,
-constitute an inconsistent premiss-set. He added, of course, that the inconsistency might be removed by substituting alternative and perhaps more subtle analyses, but cautioned that such replacements of (P1) and (P2) would save 'ordinary theism' from his charge of positive irrationality, only if true to its 'essential requirements'.2
In an earlier paper 'Problems of Evil: More Advice to
Christian Philosophers',3 I underscored Macki it a step further. In debates about whether th evil can establish the irrationality of religiou be taken, both by the atheologians who de believers who defend against it, to insure t attribute-analyses accurately reflect that relig of Divine power and goodness. It does the ath to argue for the falsity of Christianity on th existence of an omnipotent, omniscient, pleas incompossible with a world such as ours, b never believed God was a pleasure-maximi equally, the truth of Christianity woul defended by the observation that an omnip egoist could have created a world with suf because Christians insist that God loves other than Himself. The extension of'evil' in (2) in li Since Mackie and his successors are out to show that 'the several parts of the essential theological doctrine are inconsistent each other',4 they can accomplish their aim only if circumscribe the extension of 'evil' as their religious oppon do. By the same token, it is not enough for Chris philosophers to explain how the power, knowledge, goodness of God could coexist with some evils or other; a f account must exhibit the compossibility of Divine perfe with evils in the amounts and of the kinds found in the actual world (and evaluated as such by Christian standards).
The moral of my earlier story might be summarized where the internal coherence of a system of religious belief stake, successful arguments for its inconsistency must dr premisses (explicitly or implicitly) internal to that system obviously acceptable to its adherents; likewise for succ rebuttals or explanations of consistency. The thrust argument is to push both sides of the debate towards detailed attention to and subtle understanding of the reli system in question.
As a Christian philosopher, I want to focus in this paper
Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes:  1)  Marilyn McCord Adams outlines her previous response to Mackie’s article ‘Evil and Omnipotence’. Summarize her first response to Mackie’s paper. (3-4 sentences)

In her first response to Mackie's article "Evil and Omnipotence," Marilyn McCord Adams emphasized the importance of accurate representation of religious beliefs in debates about the problem of evil to determine whether it establishes the irrationality of religious faith. She argued that both atheists (atheologians) and believers must ensure that their arguments reflect an accurate understanding of religious concepts of divine power and goodness. Adams pointed out that it is fruitless for atheists to argue against Christianity by portraying a god that believers do not recognize, such as a pleasure-maximizing deity, just as it is equally essential for the truth of Christianity to portray a god who is not an egoist but one who loves creatures other than Himself. This approach aims to ensure that discussions on the problem of evil genuinely address the theological concepts at stake rather than straw man representations.
include quotes

In her first response to Mackie's article, Marilyn McCord Adams underlined the necessity for both supporters and critics of religious faith to engage sincerely with the actual doctrines of the faith when discussing the problem of evil. She stated, "It does the atheologian no good to argue for the falsity of Christianity on the grounds that the existence of an omnipotent, omniscient, pleasure-maximizer is incompossible with a world such as ours, because Christians have never believed God was a pleasure-maximizer." Moreover, she highlighted the critical aspect of properly understanding divine characteristics by adding, "equally, the truth of Christianity would not be saved or shown by observation that an omnipotent egoist could have created a world with sufficient goods in it because Christians insist that God loves creatures other than Himself." Adams emphasized the importance of this approach by arguing that to effectively debate the problem of evil, both sides must ensure their "attribute-analyses accurately reflect that religious system's understanding of Divine power and goodness." Through these quotes, Adams makes it clear that a meaningful discussion on the problem of evil must be rooted in an accurate and nuanced understanding of the religious beliefs under examination.

problem for the truth of Christianity raised by what I shall call 'horrendous' evils. Although our world is riddled with them, the Biblical record punctuated by them, and one of them-viz., the passion of Christ, according to Christian belief, the judicial murder of God by the people of God-is memorialized by the Church on its most solemn holiday (Good Friday) and in its central sacrament (the Eucharist), the problem of horrendous evils is largely skirted by standard treatments for the good reason that they are intractable by them. After showing why, I will draw on other Christian materials to sketch ways of meeting this, the deepest of religious problems.
II
Defining the Category. For present purposes, I define 'horrendous evils' as 'evils the participation in (the doing or suffering of) which gives one reason prima facie to doubt whether one's life could (given their inclusion in it) be a great good to one on the whole'. Such reasonable doubt arises because it is so difficult humanly to conceive how such evils could be overco Borrowing Chisholm's contrast between balancing off (w occurs when the opposing values of mutually exclusive parts whole partially or totally cancel each other out) and de (which cannot occur by the mere addition to the whole of a part of opposing value, but involves some 'organic unity' am the values of parts and wholes, as when the positive aest value of a whole painting defeats the ugliness of a small col patch)5, horrendous evils seem primafacie, not only to balanc but to engulf the positive value of a participant's l Nevertheless, that very horrendous proportion, by which t threaten to rob a person's life of positive meaning, cries out only to be engulfed, but to be made meaningful through pos and decisive defeat.
I understand this criterion to be objective, but rela individuals. The example of habitual complainers, who how to make the worst of a good situation, shows individ to be incorrigible experts on what ills would defeat the value of their lives. Nevertheless, nature and experience people with different strengths; one bears easily what crushes another. And a major consideration in dete individual's life is/has been a great good whole, is invariably and appropriately h him/her.6
I offer the following list of paradigmatic woman and axing off of her arms, psychop ultimate goal is the disintegration of pe one's deepest loyalties, cannibalizing one's o abuse of the sort described by Ivan Karam phy, parental incest, slow death by starvat the Nazi death camps, the explosion of populated areas, having to choose which of live and which be executed by terrorists and/or unwitting agent of the disfigurem one loves best. I regard these as paradigm most people would find in the doing or suf facie reason to doubt the positive mea Christian belief counts the crucifixion of C one hand, death by crucifixion seem Messianic vocation; for according to Je hanging from a tree made its victim definitively excluded from the compass of disqualified from being the Messiah. O represented the defeat of its perpetrators' as those who were to prepare the people of coming, kill and ritually accurse the true later theological understanding, God Him
III
The Impotence of Standard Solutions. For better or worse, now-standard strategies for 'solving' the problem of powerless in the face of horrendous evils.

Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes: 2) How does Adams define ‘horrendous evils’ ? What do you think she means when she says ‘horrendous evils seem prima facie, not only to balance off but to engulf the positive value of a participant’s life’ ? (3-4 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams defines 'horrendous evils' as "evils the participation in (the doing or suffering of) which gives one reason prima facie to doubt whether one's life could (given their inclusion in it) be a great good to one on the whole." When she states that "horrendous evils seem prima facie, not only to balance off but to engulf the positive value of a participant’s life," Adams means that these types of evils are so profoundly disturbing and damaging that they don't simply negate some good in a person's life; instead, they appear to overwhelm and consume any sense of value or meaning that one's life might have. These evils go beyond creating a balance of good and bad experiences to the point where they threaten the very possibility of seeing life as a positive and meaningful whole.

The Insufficiency of Global Defeat. A pair order here: (i) between two dimensions of relation to creation-viz., 'producer of 'goodness to' or 'love of individual create between the overbalance/defeat of evil by scale, and the overbalance/defeat of evil b context of an individual person's life.'2 Co may separate two problems of evil parallel to goodness mentioned in (i).
In effect, generic and global approaches a first problem: they defend Divine goodn (global) dimension by suggesting logically for the global defeat of evils. But establishin a producer of global goods does not auto second problem, especially in a world cont evils. For God cannot be said to be good or lov persons the positive meaning of whose liv engulfed in and/or defeated by evils--that is whose lives horrendous evils remain undefea way unsupplemented global and generic appr to explain the latter, would be by apply reasons-why to particular cases of horrendou
Unfortunately, such an exercise fails to Suppose for the sake of argument that horr included in maximally perfect world orders; constitutive of such an order would assign it global positive meaning. But would knowle defeat for a mother the prima facie reaso cannibalism of her own infant, to wish that born? Again, the aim of perfect retributi meaning on evils imposed. But would kno torturer was being tortured give the victim w turned traitor under pressure, any more reas life worthwhile? Would it not merely multi torturer to doubt that his/her life could turn him/her on the whole? Could the truck-driv runs over his beloved child find consolation in the idea that this middle-known'" but unintended side-effect was part of price God accepted for a world with the best balance of mor good over moral evil He could get?
Not only does the application to horrors of such generic a global reasons for Divine permission of evils fail to solve second problem of evil; it makes it worse by adding generic pr facie reasons to doubt whether human life would be a great go to individual human beings in possible worlds where suc Divine motives were operative. For, taken in isolation and ma to bear the weight of the whole explanation, such reasons-w draw a picture of Divine indifference or even hostility to th human plight. Would the fact that God permitted horr because they were constitutive means to His end of glob perfection, or that He tolerated them because He could obtai that global end anyway, make the participant's life mor tolerable, more worth living for him/her? Given radical hu vulnerability to horrendous evils, the ease with which huma participate in them, whether as victim or perpetrator, would the thought that God visits horrors on anyone who caused th simply because s/he deserves it, provide one more reason expect human life to be a nightmare?
Those willing to split the two problems of evil apart migh adopt a divide-and-conquer strategy, by simply denying Divi goodness along the second dimension. For example, man Christians do not believe that God will insure an overwhelmin good life to each and every person He creates. Some say t decisive defeat of evil with good is promised only within the li of the obedient, who enter by the narrow gate. Some specula that the elect may be few. Many recognize that the sufferings this present life are as nothing compared to the hell of eter torment, designed to defeat goodness with horrors within t lives of the damned. Such a road can be consistently travelle toll of admitting that human life in worlds bet. Imagine (adapting Rawls' device) pers position, considering possible worlds con differing power, wisdom, and character, an fates. The question they are to answer whether they would willingly enter it as a behind a veil of ignorance as to which occupy. Reason would, I submit, dictate a n worlds whose omniscient and omnipotent mortem horrors that remain undefeated within the context of the human participant's life; afortiori, for worlds in which so or most humans suffer eternal torment.
Inaccessible Reasons. So far, I have argued that generic a global solutions are at best incomplete: however well t account of Divine motivating reasons deals with the fi problem of evil, the attempt to extend it to the second fail making it worse. This verdict might seem primafacie tolerab standard generic and global approaches and indicative of onl minor modification in their strategy: let the above-mentio generic and global reasons cover Divine permission of horrendous evils, and find other reasons compatible with per goodness why even an omnipotent, omniscient God wo permit horrors.
In my judgment, such an approach is hopeless. As Plantin points out, where horrendous evils are concerned, not only we not know God's actual reason for permitting them; we ca even conceive of any plausible candidate sort of reason consis with worthwhile lives for human participants in them.
IV
The How of God's Victory. Up to now my discussion has given reader cause to wonder whose side I am on anyway? For I ha insisted, with rebels like Ivan Karamazov andJohn Stuart Mi on spot-lighting the problem horrendous evils pose. Yet, I h signalled my preference for a vision of Christianity that insists o both dimensions of Divine goodness, and maintains not only
Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes: 3) At the top of p. 302 Adams makes this pair of distinctions:

(i) God as ‘producer of global goods’ / God as ‘goodness to’ individual persons 
and 
(ii) ‘overbalance/defeat’ of evil on a global scale / ‘overbalance/defeat’ of evil on an individual scale

and she thinks that many solutions to the problem of evil focus on the first element of these two pairs (i.e., the global).

What does she think are some of the problems of this focus on the first element of the pairs (God as producer of global goods; overbalance or defeat of evil on a global scale)? (pp. 303-304) (2-3 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams points out that focusing solely on the first elements of the pairs (God as 'producer of global goods' and the 'overbalance/defeat' of evil on a global scale) fails to address the personal and individual aspect of evil's impact. She argues that establishing God as a producer of global goods does not automatically resolve the problem of horrendous evils for individuals, especially in a world containing such evils. Adams suggests that this global focus cannot sufficiently explain or justify the personal experiences of horrendous evils, as "God cannot be said to be good or loving towards persons the positive meaning of whose lives are engulfed in and/or defeated by evils—that is, towards those whose lives horrendous evils remain undefeated in a way unsupplemented global and generic approaches to explaining the latter" would be. This emphasis on global solutions, therefore, inadequately addresses the intimate and devastating effects of horrendous evils on individual lives.

that God will be good enough to created persons to make human life a good bet, but also (b) that each created person will have a life that is a great good to him/her on the whole. My critique of standard approaches to the problem of evil thus seems to reinforce atheologian Mackie's verdict of 'positive irrationality' for such a religious position.
4.1 Whys versus Hows. The inaccessibility of reasons-why seems especially  decisive. For surely an all-wise and all-powerful God, who loved each created person enough (a) to defeat any experienced horrors within the context of the participant's life, and (b) to give each created person a life that is a great good to him/her on the whole, would not permit such persons to suffer horrors for no reason.'" Does not our inability even to conceive of plausible candidate reasons suffice to make belief in such a God positively irrational in a world containing horrors? In my judgment, it does not.
To be sure, motivating reasons come in several varieties relative to our conceptual grasp: There are (i) reasons of the sort we can readily understand when we are informed of them (e.g., the mother who permits her child to undergo painful heart surgery because it is the only humanly possible way to save its life). Moreover, there are (ii) reasons we would be cognitively, emotionally, and spiritually equipped to grasp if only we had a larger memory or wider attention span (analogy: I may be able to memorize small town street plans; memorizing the road networks of the entire country is a task requiring more of the same, in the way that proving G6del's theorem is not). Some generic and global approaches insinuate that Divine permission of evils has motivating reasons of this sort. Finally, (iii) there are reasons that we are cognitively, emotionally, and/or spiritually too immature to fathom (the way a two-year old child is incapable of understanding its mother's reasons for permitting the surgery). I agree with Plantinga that our ignorance of Divine reasons for permitting horrendous evils is not of types (i) or (ii), but of type (iii).
Nevertheless, if there are varieties of ignorance, there are also varieties of reassurance. The two-year old heart patient is convinced of its mother's love, not by her c reasons, but by her intimate care and p painful experience. The story ofJob sugges is true with human participation in horren does not give Job His reasons-why, and im smart enough to grasp them; ratherJob is l of Divine power, and sees God's goodness f I suggest, to exhibit the logical compossibili of Divine goodness with horrendous sufferi to find logically possible reasons why God is enough to show how God can be good persons despite their participation in h them within the context of the indi giving that individual a life that is a great the whole.
What sort of Valuables? In my opinion, the reasonableness 
Christianity can be maintained in the face of horrendous ev only by drawing on resources of religious value theory. For way for God to be good to created persons is by relating th appropriately to relevant and great goods. But philosoph and religious theories differ importantly on what valuables t admit into their ontology. Some maintain that 'what you se what you get', but nevertheless admit a wide range of valuab from sensory pleasures, the beauty of nature and cultu artifacts, the joys of creativity, to loving personal intim Others posit a transcendent good (e.g. the Form of the Good Platonism, or God, the Supremely Valuable Object, Christianity). In the spirit of Ivan Karamazov, I am convinc that the depth of horrific evil cannot be accurately estimat without recognizing it to be incommensurate with any pack of merely non-transcendent goods and so unable to be balan off, much less defeated thereby.
Where the internal coherence of Christianity is the is however, it is fair to appeal to its own store of valuables. Fr Christian point of view, God is a being a greater than w cannot be conceived, a good incommensurate with both crea goods and temporal evils. Likewise, the good of beatific, face face intimacy with God is simply incommensurate with merely non-transcendent goods or ills a person might experie


Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes: 4) In section 4.1 (pp. 305-306) of this article, Adams discusses issues surrounding our cognitive powers and their limitations and the possibility of there being reassurance in the midst of suffering without having ‘reasons-why’ or explanations for the necessity of suffering. What image does she offer for how one might understand the compatibility between lack of ‘reasons-why’ suffering occurs and some type of reassurance? How does she think this image aligns with the story of the Book of Job? (2-3 sentences)

In section 4.1 of her article, Marilyn McCord Adams uses the image of a two-year-old child undergoing heart surgery to illustrate how one might understand the compatibility between the lack of 'reasons-why' suffering occurs and some type of reassurance. She argues that just as the child is convinced of its mother's love not through understanding the complex reasons behind the painful experience but through the mother's intimate care and presence, individuals can find reassurance in God's goodness in a similar way. Adams aligns this image with the story of the Book of Job by suggesting that just as God did not provide Job with reasons-why for his suffering but rather revealed His divine power and presence, so too can individuals sense God's goodness and love amidst horrendous evils, not through comprehending specific reasons but through experiencing a relationship with the divine.
include quotes

In discussing the issue of cognitive limitations and the possibility of reassurance amidst suffering without clear 'reasons-why', Marilyn McCord Adams offers a poignant analogy: "Moreover, there are (iii) reasons that we are cognitively, emotionally, and/or spiritually too immature to fathom (the way a two-year old child is incapable of understanding its mother's reasons for permitting the surgery)." She further explicates this image's relation to the story of Job, stating, "The story of Job suggests the same is true with human participation in horrendous evils. God does not give Job His reasons-why, and implies that Job is not smart enough to grasp them; rather Job is left reassured of Divine power, and sees God's goodness for himself." Through these quotes, Adams illustrates her view that individuals can be reassured of God's goodness, akin to a child assured by a parent's love through presence and care, rather than through comprehending the complex reasons behind their suffering. This reassurance, she contends, aligns with the biblically depicted experience of Job, who found solace not in understanding but in witnessing the manifest presence of God's power and goodness.

Thus, the good of beatific face-to-face intimacy with God wo engulf(in a sense analogous to Chisholmian balancing off) ev the horrendous evils humans experience in this present life h below, and overcome any prima facie reasons the individ had to doubt whether his/her life would or could be worth
living.
Personal Meaning, Horrors Defeated. Engulfing personal horrors within the context of the participant's life would vouchsafe to that individual a life that was a great good to him/her on the whole. I am still inclined to think it would guarantee that immeasurable Divine goodness to any person thus benefited. But there is good theological reason for Christians to believe that God would go further, beyond engulfment to defeat. For it is the nature of persons to look for meaning, both in their lives and in the world. Divine respect for and commitment to created personhood would drive God to make all those sufferings which threaten to destroy the positive meaning of a person's life meaningful through positive defeat.
How could God do it? So far as I can see, only by integrating participation in horrendous evils into a person's relationship with God. Possible dimensions of integration are charted by Christian soteriology. I pause here to sketch three'6. Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes: 
5) What does Adams think might ‘engulf’ horrendous evils, and how would it do so? (p. 307) (2-3 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams believes that the good of beatific, face-to-face intimacy with God would 'engulf' horrendous evils. She explains this by saying, "the good of beatific face-to-face intimacy with God would engulf (in a sense analogous to Chisholmian balancing off) even the horrendous evils humans experience in this present life from below, and overcome any prima facie reasons the individual had to doubt whether his/her life would or could be worth living." According to Adams, it is through this profound and personal intimacy with the divine that the overwhelming and destructive power of horrendous evils can be reconciled and overcome, offering individuals reassurance that their lives can still be perceived as a great good overall, despite the horrors they have endured.

Thus, the good of beatific face-to-face intimacy with God wo engulf(in a sense analogous to Chisholmian balancing off) ev the horrendous evils humans experience in this present life h below, and overcome any prima facie reasons the individ had to doubt whether his/her life would or could be worth
living.
Personal Meaning, Horrors Defeated. Engulfing personal horrors within the context of the participant's life would vouchsafe to that individual a life that was a great good to him/her on the whole. I am still inclined to think it would guarantee that immeasurable Divine goodness to any person thus benefited. But there is good theological reason for Christians to believe that God would go further, beyond engulfment to defeat. For it is the nature of persons to look for meaning, both in their lives and in the world. Divine respect for and commitment to created personhood would drive God to make all those sufferings which threaten to destroy the positive meaning of a person's life meaningful through positive defeat.
How could God do it? So far as I can see, only by integrating participation in horrendous evils into a person's relationship with God. Possible dimensions of integration are charted by Christian soteriology. I pause here to sketch three'6. (i) First, because God in Christ participated in horrendous evil through His passion and death, human experience of horrors can be a means of identifying with Christ, either through sympathetic identification (in which each person suffers his/her own pains, but their similarity enables each to know what it is like for the other) or through mystical identification (in which the created person is supposed literally to experience a share of Christ's
pain"). (ii) Julian of Norwich's description suggests the possible defeat of horrendous gratitude. But, just as (according to Ru experience Divine presence now as tremend and anxiety), now as fascinans (with ine perhaps our deepest suffering as much as themselves be direct visions into the inner but somehow less obscure in proportion to t a face-to-face vision of God is a good for hu with any non-transcendent goods or ills, s (including horrendous suffering) would insofar as it is a vision of God (even if it ha as it is horrendous suffering). For the most recognized as experiences of God (any more recognizes his visual image of a brown Beulah the cow in the distance). But, Christ claim, at least from the post-mortem persp vision, such sufferings will be seen for retrospectively no one will wish away any 


with God from his/her life-history of this world. The creat person's experience of the beatific vision together with his/ knowledge that intimate Divine presence stretched back o his/her pre-mortem life and reached down into the depths o his/her worst suffering, would provide retrospective comfo independent of comprehension of the reasons-why akin to t two-year-old's assurance of its mother's love. Taking this thi approach, Christians would not need to commit themsel about what in any event we do not know: viz., whether we w (like the two-year-old) ever grow up enough to understand t reasons why God permits our participation in horrendous ev For by contrast with the best of earthly mothers, such Divi intimacy is an incommensurate good and would cancel out fo the creature any need to know why.
V
Conclusion. The worst evils demand to be defeated by the be goods. Horrendous evils can be overcome only by the goodne of God. Relative to human nature, participation in horrendo evils and loving intimacy with God are alike disproportionat for the former threatens to engulf the good in an indivi human life with evil, while the latter guarantees the reve engulfment of evil by good. Relative to one another, there is disproportion, because the good that God is, and intima relationship with Him, is incommensurate with created g and evils alike. Because intimacy with God so outscales relati (good or bad) with any creatures, integration into the hu person's relationship with God confers significant meaning positive value even on horrendous suffering. This result coh with basic Christian intuition: that the powers of darkness a stronger than humans, but they are no match for God!
Standard generic and global solutions have for the most p tried to operate within the territory common to believer and believer, within the confines ofreligion-neutral value theory. Man discussions reflect the hope that substitute attribute-analyses, can didate reasons-why and/or defeaters could issue out of va shared by believers and unbelievers alike. And some virtually m this a requirement on an adequate solution. Mackie knew bet how to distinguish the many charges that may be levelled aga religion. Just as philosophers may or may not find the existe 

Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes:6) What are the three possible scenarios Adams gives for illustrating how God might ‘defeat’ horrendous evils without necessarily providing ‘reasons-why’ or explanations for suffering? (pp. 307-309) (3-4 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams suggests three possible scenarios illustrating how God might ‘defeat’ horrendous evils:

1. "**Identifying with Christ**" - Adams explains that because God in Christ has participated in horrendous evil through His passion and death, human experiences of horrors can serve as a means of identifying with Christ. This can happen either "through sympathetic identification" where each person, by enduring their own pains, can understand what it is like for the other, or "through mystical identification" where the believer could literally share in a portion of Christ's pain.

2. "**Julian of Norwich's concept of post-mortem gratitude**" - In reference to Julian of Norwich, Adams points to the idea that at the end of time, creatures will thank God for everything, including their sufferings. Julian's vision suggests that "the creature's experience of Divine love will issue in such full and unending joy as could not be imagined in all the sea of human pain and suffering" thus proposing a potential defeat of horrendous evils through a profound, retrospective gratitude.

3. "**Temporal sufferings as participation in the inner life of God**" - Adams considers the possibility that temporal suffering might be meaningfully integrated into the inner life of God, perhaps suggesting that, contrary to medieval theology, God possesses capacities for sorrow and joy. This scenario presents the idea that suffering, and indeed even our deepest sufferings, might be "direct visions into the inner trinitarian life," offering a form of consolation that does not clarify the reasons for suffering but embeds it within a context of divine intimacy.

Adams presents these scenarios not as definitive explanations but as ways to conceptualize how a believer might find their horrendous sufferings to be positively defeated through a transformative relationship with God, without the necessity of understanding explicit reasons for their pain.

Answer question and include quotes:6) What are the three possible scenarios Adams gives for illustrating how God might ‘defeat’ horrendous evils without necessarily providing ‘reasons-why’ or explanations for suffering? (pp. 307-309) (3-4 sentences)

In her exploration of how God might 'defeat' horrendous evils, Marilyn McCord Adams proposes three scenarios:

1. **Identifying with Christ**: Adams suggests, "because God in Christ participated in horrendous evil through His passion and death, human experience of horrors can be a means of identifying with Christ," either through sympathetic or mystical identification. This concept allows individuals to link their suffering with Christ's, providing a form of spiritual solidarity and meaning.

2. **Julian of Norwich's post-mortem gratitude**: Referencing Julian of Norwich, Adams mentions the idea that creatures will thank God for all experiences, including suffering, after their earthly life. This is because "the creature's experience of Divine love will issue in such full and unending joy as could not be imagined in all the sea of human pain and suffering," suggesting a perspective where past horrors are overwhelmed by divine love.

3. **Temporal sufferings as participation in the inner life of God**: Adams contemplates that "temporal suffering might be integrated into the inner life of God," making it part of a divine relationship where suffering becomes a way to share in God's own experiences. This integration provides a form of deep connection to God, transforming the meaning of suffering.

Adams presents these scenarios as conceptual paths for how the deeply personal and profoundly difficult experiences of evil might be reconciled with a loving and powerful God, without necessitating a clear intellectual explanation for why such suffering occurs.
(3-4 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams outlines three conceptual frameworks through which God might 'defeat' horrendous evils without necessarily providing explicit 'reasons-why' for the suffering:

1. **Through identification with Christ**: She suggests that "because God in Christ participated in horrendous evil through His passion and death, human experience of horrors can be a means of identifying with Christ," either in a sympathetic or mystical manner. This approach leverages the shared experience of suffering as a means of spiritual connection.

2. **Via Julian of Norwich’s vision of post-mortem gratitude**: Adams references Julian of Norwich's view that at the end-times, individuals will thank God for all experiences, including sufferings, because "the creature's experience of Divine love will issue in such full and unending joy as could not be imagined in all the sea of human pain and suffering." This scenario suggests a future recognition and gratitude that retrospectively transforms understanding and feelings about past sufferings.

3. **By integrating temporal sufferings into the inner life of God**: Adams explores the idea that personal suffering may be understood as participation in God's own deep experience, thus allowing "temporal suffering...to be developed into a face-to-face vision of God." This perspective provides a way to see suffering not as a pointless horror but as a complex facet of a profound relationship with the divine, where even painful experiences have a place.

These frameworks offer insights into how a belief system might reconcile the existence of deeply disturbing evils with a compassionately involved deity by reframing the meaning and significance of suffering itself.

Firstly, she discusses the idea of **identifying with Christ**, suggesting that shared suffering can lead to a spiritual solidarity with Christ's own sufferings. Secondly, she mentions **Julian of Norwich's vision of post-mortem gratitude**, where beings might retrospectively thank God for their sufferings due to the overwhelming joy experienced in divine love, transforming their view of past horrors. Lastly, Adams explores the notion of **temporal sufferings as participation in the inner life of God**, positing that suffering might offer an intimate connection with the divine, thereby imbuing it with profound meaning and value.

Answer question and include quotes:6) What are the three possible scenarios Adams gives for illustrating how God might ‘defeat’ horrendous evils without necessarily providing ‘reasons-why’ or explanations for suffering? (pp. 307-309) (3-4 sentences)

Marilyn McCord Adams discusses three scenarios for how God might 'defeat' horrendous evils, focusing on integrating these evils into a person’s relationship with God as a means of overcoming their negative impact:

1. Through **Identifying with Christ**: Adams explains that humans can find a means of identifying with Christ, "either through sympathetic identification (in which each person suffers his/her own pains, but their similarity enables each to know what it is like for the other) or through mystical identification (in which the created person is supposed literally to experience a share of Christ’s pain)." This identification with Christ’s suffering offers a way to find meaning in suffering.

2. Via **Julian of Norwich’s concept of post-mortem gratitude**: Adams points to Julian of Norwich's vision where "the creature’s experience of Divine love... could not be imagined in all the sea of human pain and suffering..." suggesting that a retrospective gratitude for experiences of suffering, understood within the context of God's overarching plan of love, could defeat the detrimental effects of those evils.

3. By understanding **temporal sufferings as participation in the inner life of God**: She proposes that human suffering might be integrated into "the inner life of God," which would transform the nature of these sufferings. This perspective posits that engaging with our sufferings as part of a shared experience with God could provide them with a positive meaning, suggesting that "perhaps our deepest suffering...themselves be direct visions into the inner trinitarian life."

These scenarios do not offer direct 'reasons-why' for the existence of suffering, but rather illustrate ways in which faith and a deepened relationship with God can transform the nature of suffering from something wholly negative to an aspect of the divine-human relationship that has intrinsic value and meaning.

Marilyn McCord Adams outlines three methods through which God might 'defeat' horrendous evils, focusing on their integration into one's relationship with the Divine, rather than providing explicit 'reasons-why':

1. **Identifying with Christ**: "either through sympathetic identification... or through mystical identification," where personal suffering aligns with Christ's, offering a shared understanding of pain and a pathway to overcome it through spiritual communion.

2. **Julian of Norwich’s Post-Mortem Gratitude**: Adams references Julian's vision where, in the afterlife, one might thank God for all experiences, including suffering, because the divine experience "will issue in such full and unending joy as could not be imagined in all the sea of human pain and suffering," suggesting a retrospective transformation of understanding.

3. **Temporal Sufferings as Participation in God's Inner Life**: Proposing that "temporal suffering might be integrated into the inner life of God," Adams suggests a profound form of solidarity with the Divine, where human suffering is not just shared but is an essential aspect of a deeper, mystical union with God's own experiences.

These scenarios represent speculative but deeply hopeful theological reflections on how belief in a loving and omnipotent God can be reconciled with the existence of significant suffering, by transforming the meaning of that suffering through a deeply intimate relationship with God.

Marilyn McCord Adams proposes three ways God might address the issue of 'horrendous evils' without providing clear-cut explanations for their existence:

1. **Identifying with Christ**: She suggests participation in horrendous evils can become a means for "identifying with Christ, either through sympathetic identification...or through mystical identification," where personal sufferings echo Christ's, offering a form of spiritual solace and connection.

2. **Post-mortem Gratitude According to Julian of Norwich**: Adams references Julian of Norwich, stating the eventual realization that "the creature's experience of Divine love will issue in such full and unending joy as could not be imagined in all the sea of human pain and suffering," illustrating how ultimate divine love may retrospectively justify individual sufferings.

3. **Integration into God's Inner Life**: Finally, she proposes that "temporal suffering might be developed into a face-to-face vision of God," suggesting a profound communal suffering with God that imbues personal hardships with significant, transformative meaning within the divine relationship.

These scenarios articulate a theological perspective wherein the seemingly insurmountable weight of human suffering is approached not through rational explanation but through deepened relational and experiential ties to the divine.

Marilyn McCord Adams, in discussing how God might 'defeat' horrendous evils, offers three insightful scenarios that move beyond the need for explicit 'reasons-why' sufferings occur. First, she suggests that through "identifying with Christ, either through sympathetic identification...or through mystical identification," individuals can find profound spiritual connection and solace in their sufferings by aligning their experiences with those of Christ's passion and death. Secondly, drawing from Julian of Norwich, Adams highlights the potential for post-mortem understanding where believers might realize that "the creature's experience of Divine love will issue in such full and unending joy as could not be imagined in all the sea of human pain and suffering," suggesting that divine love ultimately transforms and transcends human anguish. Lastly, she proposes the idea that "temporal suffering might be developed into a face-to-face vision of God," arguing that personal adversities could deepen one's relationship with the divine, converting pain into a pivotal element of spiritual communion. Through these scenarios, Adams invites a theological reflection that sees horrendous evils not as obstacles to faith but as complex facets of a profound, transformative engagement with the divine mystery.

Read above text from Marilyn McCord Adams, ‘Horrendous Evils and the Goodness of God’ . Answer question and include quotes: 1) Marilyn McCord Adams outlines her previous response to Mackie’s article ‘Evil and Omnipotence’. Summarize her first response to Mackie’s paper. (3-4 sentences)

In her response to J.L. Mackie's article 'Evil and Omnipotence,' Marilyn McCord Adams emphasizes the importance of accurately reflecting the religious understanding of divine attributes in discussions on the problem of evil. She criticizes arguments that oversimplify or misrepresent the nature of God—as either a pleasure-maximizer or an omnipotent egoist—as they do not align with the Christian belief that God is loving towards creatures beyond Himself. Adams argues for a nuanced approach that respects the complexities of Christian theology, suggesting that debates about the problem of evil need to consider the 'attribute-analyses' that are true to the religious system's portrayal of God's power and goodness. She asserts that for criticisms of Christianity based on the existence of evil to be valid, they must engage with the religion's core doctrines and not straw man versions of them.
